title: Life expectancy, including UN projections
subtitle: The [period life expectancy](#dod:period-life-expectancy) at birth. This includes the observed life expectancy since 1950, and the medium-variant projections for the future, based on estimates by the UN Population Division.
caption:
Life expectancy, including UN projections

The period life expectancy at birth. This includes the observed life expectancy since 1950, and the medium-variant projections for the future, based on estimates by the UN Population Division.

This line chart tracks period life expectancy at birth from 1950 through 2100, combining observed historical data with medium-variant projections. The vertical axis ranges from 0 to 100 years, while the horizontal axis spans seven decades of history and eight decades of future estimates. Five distinct trajectories are plotted: Japan, Sweden, Brazil, India, and the World average. In 1950, India begins at the lowest point, near 40 years, while Japan starts around 60 years. Sweden and the World average sit in the mid-range, between 45 and 50 years. By 2020, Japan and Sweden have risen to the top of the chart, exceeding 80 years, with Japan showing a slight dip around that year before resuming its upward trend. Brazil and the World average converge near 70 to 75 years by 2020, having climbed steadily from their 1950 positions. India’s line shows the steepest overall increase, rising from below 40 to approximately 70 years by 2020. Beyond 2020, all lines transition to dotted projections. Japan’s projected path continues upward, approaching 95 years by 2100. Sweden follows closely, nearing 90 years. Brazil and India continue climbing but remain below the global average projection, which itself rises to about 85 years by 2100. The World line remains consistently above India and Brazil throughout the period, reflecting its composite nature. All projected lines maintain positive slopes, indicating continued gains in life expectancy across all represented entities through the end of the century.
